user_query:
I’m organizing a short weekly planning call for fans to celebrate and share news about my husband Steve Whitmire’s work. Please build and run a small automation that, each week, searches the web for new items related to Steve, ranks the findings, and prepares meeting notes with clear action items assigned to our small volunteer group. Then set it up so it can be scheduled to run every Monday at 09:00 local time.

Requirements
1) Inputs (already provided in input/):
- input/attendees.csv — use this to create the attendee checklist and to assign action items by role.
- input/search_topics.yaml — use these queries and keyword rules to drive the searches and action item classification.
- input/agenda_template.md — use this template to render the meeting notes by replacing the placeholders indicated in the file.

2) Implement a command-line script (choose a language you’re comfortable with) at scripts/weekly_whitmire_digest.py that does the following when invoked:
- Perform search engine queries from the Internet using at least two different public web search engines (your choice) for each query listed under queries in input/search_topics.yaml. Do not hardcode specific URLs into the deliverable; instead, choose the engines at run time and record their names. Collect up to ranking.per_result_limit results per query per engine.
- For each search result, capture at minimum: query_term, engine_name, engine_rank (1-based), title, url. Compute a simple score for each result: score = base_score_for_rank_1 - (engine_rank - 1) + (title_keyword_bonus * count of query keywords appearing in the title, case-insensitive) + primary_name_bonus if the title contains "Steve" or "Whitmire". Use the numeric values from input/search_topics.yaml.ranking.
- Filter and deduplicate results by normalized URL (domain + path, strip tracking/query parameters). Keep the highest-scored copy when duplicates occur.
- Sort the final set by score descending, then by engine_rank ascending as a tiebreaker. Produce both the full ranked list and the top 10.
- Classify each top-10 item into one of three buckets to drive action items using input/search_topics.yaml.classification_keywords: if the title contains any to_contact keyword → To contact; else if it contains any to_share keyword → To share; else → To review.
- Assign action items in a round-robin manner to attendees from input/attendees.csv by role: To contact → attendees with role Outreach; To share → role Social; To review → role Research. If a bucket has more items than matching attendees, continue cycling through them. Include the assignee’s email in the action line.
- Render meeting notes by populating input/agenda_template.md placeholders with:
  - date as today’s date in YYYY-MM-DD;
  - query_count = number of queries attempted;
  - engine_list = a comma-separated list of engine names used;
  - result_count = total number of de-duplicated results kept;
  - top_items_table = a concise table (or bullet list) of the top 10 with rank, title, url, score, and classification;
  - action_items = a checklist where each line includes the classification, a short description based on the title, and the assigned attendee name and email;
  - attendee_checklist = a checklist of all attendees by name.
- Write logs for the run, including which engines were used and the exact query strings executed.

3) Outputs (create these paths if they don’t exist):
- out/search/YYYY-MM-DD/results.csv — all de-duplicated results with fields: query_term,engine_name,engine_rank,title,url,score.
- out/search/YYYY-MM-DD/top10.csv — the top 10 after ranking and de-duplication with the same fields plus classification.
- out/search/YYYY-MM-DD/queries.json — a JSON document noting the query terms attempted, the engines used, timestamp, and the per-query counts of results collected before and after de-duplication.
- out/notes/meeting_notes_YYYY-MM-DD.md — the rendered meeting notes populated from the template.
- out/logs/run_YYYY-MM-DD_HHMMSS.log — a human-readable log of the run.

4) Scheduling deliverables (do not execute a system-wide install in this environment; instead, produce ready-to-use artifacts):
- scripts/schedule_weekly.sh — a POSIX shell script that, when run on a Unix-like system, installs (or updates) a crontab entry to run scripts/weekly_whitmire_digest.py every Monday at 09:00 local time and append logs to out/logs/cron.log. The script should be idempotent.
- scripts/register_weekly_task.ps1 — a PowerShell script that registers (or updates) an equivalent Windows Task Scheduler task to run weekly on Monday at 09:00, logging to out/logs/schtask.log. The script should be idempotent and include parameters for working directory and script path.
- out/schedule/next_run.txt — a one-line summary computed locally by your code stating the next Monday at 09:00 local time the task would run (ISO timestamp) and the command that will be invoked on both platforms.

5) Run it once now to demonstrate: After implementing the script, execute it once to produce today’s artifacts under out/ as specified above, using the provided input files and live Internet search engine queries.

Verification notes
- I will verify structure and completeness by checking that outputs exist at the exact paths above and that counts in out/search/YYYY-MM-DD/queries.json and out/logs correspond to the CSVs produced. I won’t check specific article content, but the ranking, filtering, and assignment logic must be reflected in the outputs.

Thank you for helping me keep our notes organized and actionable while honoring Steve’s ongoing legacy! All file paths are relative to the workspace directory.
